original_abstract:
Iron (Fe) is a fundamental cornerstone of terrestrial life, yet in the human body, it exists in a precarious equilibrium where sufficiency is vital and excess is toxic. This delicate balance is mirrored by other essential and non-essential elements, including calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg), and a variety of trace metals such as copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), and nickel (Ni). Many of these elements share common protein pathways for absorption and transport, leading to complex physiological interactions. For instance, copper and cadmium are known to inhibit iron absorption, whereas an overabundance of iron can antagonize copper metabolism and suppress ceruloplasmin levels. Furthermore, excessive iron can impede zinc uptake, while transferrin serves as a shared carrier for both zinc and nickel.

Calcium exerts a dose-dependent inhibitory effect on divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1), thereby reducing iron absorption, while low magnesium levels have been shown to exacerbate iron deficiency symptoms. Toxic metals also disrupt this homeostasis: lead competitively interferes with iron distribution, and elevated cadmium levels significantly reduce iron uptake. Mercury exposure is frequently correlated with heightened ferritin concentrations, and nickel is known to alter intracellular iron processing. Clinical observations in hemochromatosis patients undergoing phlebotomy reveal that iron removal can trigger an increase in circulating lead and cadmium, just as trace element profiles shift during various forms of anemia. Despite these known interdependencies, the long-term physiological consequences of chronic exposure to these trace elements remain insufficiently understood.
humanized_abstract:
Iron (Fe) is an essential element for life on Earth and iron homeostasis in the human body is a critical process where iron must be sufficient for required processes but insufficient for non-required processes. In fact, other essential and non-essential elements including calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg) and trace metals such as copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg) and nickel (Ni) share many common proteins for their intestinal absorption and intracellular transport. For example, Cu2+ and Cd2+ inhibit iron absorption while high iron levels inhibit copper metabolism and decrease ceruloplasmin. High iron levels also inhibit zinc intestinal uptake. Transferrin is also a common carrier for zinc and nickel.

Calcium in a dose dependent manner inhibits divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) and thereby affects iron absorption. Low magnesium is a contributing factor to iron deficiency. The toxic metals lead, cadmium, mercury and nickel all impair iron homeostasis. In more detail, lead affects iron distribution and increased cadmium levels reduce iron uptake. Mercury is often found in elevated ferritin levels in the blood. Nickel has an impact on the cellular iron metabolism. Lead and cadmium levels increase in the blood of haemochromatosis patients undergoing phlebotomy to reduce iron levels, and similar changes in trace element levels have been observed in different forms of anaemia. The chronic effects of their interplay are not yet fully understood.
